event_blocks.summary: Amidst the sounds of an idling engine and distant birds, a man in a maroon jacket secures a blue tarp over a metal cage on a white utility truck parked beside a modest white house with peeling paint. A narrator identifies the woman with blonde hair in a bun, Kyla Jobson, a single mum holding her baby in a pink knitted sweater, as "packing up her life" due to a rental property riddled with mold and asbestos, evidenced by water damage and dark stains on the ceiling. Kyla expresses the emotional complexity of their move, feeling "excited" to escape issues like mold but also "a bit upset" about the unknown future for her kids and the baby, prompting the baby to "say goodbye to your granddad" as the man gently touches the baby’s head. The white utility truck, license plate 'TA 07 GA', revs its engine and drives away down the gravel driveway, passing a white picket fence, as Kyla watches, her expression thoughtful, and tells the baby to "Say goodbye" again. Inside, Kyla navigates a cluttered room with scattered bags, boxes, and a stroller, folding a red and teal pop-up tent, while the narrator reveals the owner wanted to raise the rent but "didn't fix the mold or the leaking roof." Kyla, carrying the baby, walks down a dimly lit hallway into a cluttered kitchen where sounds of crashing objects are heard, and she begins washing dishes with the baby on her hip, a window with a sun sticker providing natural light. A child's voice is heard asking, "You're still playing with that," and later, "I'm start getting that done." Kyla explains her unsuccessful attempts to find new housing despite applying for dozens of properties due to skyrocketing rental prices, describing the experience as "feeling like you're drowning," but concludes, "my kids are definitely worth it," just as the baby cries, prompting Kyla to respond, "Aren't they?" Later, Kyla, holding the crying baby, says, "It's just exhausting. Yes," as somber music begins and the narrator explains that having a job is no longer a guarantee of housing. Kyla expresses, "And it just feels like you're going in a circle. All I want is for you guys to have somewhere nice and safe." She places the baby into a gray stroller, and the narrator states they are moving into a motel room. Kyla, now wearing a sleeveless black top, interacts with a young child in a red shirt, Kain, at a doorway with a mesh screen, asking, "Kain, are you ready to quickly, quickly run?" Kain responds, "I'm ready," before Kyla warns, "Your feet will get wet," and Kain says, "I had a wet too," as Kyla concludes, "I'm gonna hold that."

Kyla details the 'bubbles popping up in the ceiling' and the constant 'leaks straight through' every time it rains, which the owner has failed to fix despite wanting to raise the rent.

A narrator identifies her by name and details her housing struggles.

raw_segments: The segment opens with the subtle sounds of an idling engine and distant birds, soon joined by gentle, melancholic music, as a man in a maroon jacket and green cap secures a blue tarp over a metal cage on a white utility truck. The truck is parked beside a modest white house with peeling paint and overgrown grass. A female narrator introduces the woman with blonde hair in a bun, wearing a black hoodie and jeans, holding a baby in a pink knitted sweater, as single mum Kyla Jobson, who is "packing up her life" just outside Bellingen on the New South Wales mid-north coast. Kyla's voice is heard, expressing the emotional complexity of their move, feeling both "excited" to escape issues like mold but also "a bit upset" about the unknown future for her kids and the baby. As the man interacts with Kyla, gently touching the baby’s head, Kyla prompts the baby to "say goodbye to your granddad," confirming the man's role. The man's gentle voice is heard responding, followed by a kissing sound. The white utility truck, with its visible license plate 'TA 07 GA', then revs its engine and drives away down the gravel driveway, passing a white picket fence. Kyla watches the truck depart from the side of the house, her expression thoughtful, as she tells the baby to "Say goodbye" again and "stand up there," with the sounds of the truck receding and the melancholic music continuing. On-screen text '#4Corners' and a '44' logo are visible throughout.

The video opens with an exterior shot of a modest white house, its peeling paint, weathered roof, and overgrown white picket fence establishing a sense of neglect. A narrator explains that this rental property, where Kyla lives with her baby and four-year-old, has become a health hazard, riddled with mold and asbestos. The scene transitions inside to a cluttered room where Kyla, a blonde woman in a black hoodie, holds her baby dressed in a pink knitted sweater. Kyla looks up at the ceiling, revealing significant water damage with peeling paint and dark stains. She expresses concern, telling her baby, "So you're not going in there," possibly referring to a damaged area or the tent she's about to fold. Kyla explains that the leaks started before massive floods at the start of the year, causing "bubbles popping up in the ceiling" and destroying her son's bed. She adds that "every time it rains it just leaks straight through," forcing her and both children to sleep in her room for the past four months because she "can't guarantee it's not going to rain halfway through the night" and she "just can't have my kids in here if it's like this." Amidst the disorganized room with scattered bags, boxes, and a stroller, Kyla begins to fold a red and teal pop-up tent, accompanied by rustling sounds, suggesting an attempt to organize or prepare for temporary living conditions. The narrator interjects, revealing that the owner had already told Kyla they wanted to put the rent up but "didn't fix the mold or the leaking roof." Kyla concludes, stating, "All of that's going in the bin," indicating the disposal of damaged items. The natural lighting from frosted windows highlights the atmosphere of hardship and resilience.

The video opens with Kyla, a woman with blonde hair tied in a bun, wearing a black hoodie and jeans, walking down a narrow, dimly lit hallway while carrying her baby, dressed in a pink knitted sweater and white pants. She heads towards a bright doorway, entering a cluttered kitchen where sounds of crashing and breaking objects are heard, likely from items being moved or falling within the messy space. Kyla navigates around boxes, bags, and a sink full of dishes, carefully turning on the faucet to begin washing, all while holding the calm and observant baby on her hip. Natural light streams in through a window adorned with a colorful sun sticker. A child's voice is heard intermittently, asking "You're still playing with that," and later saying "I'm start getting that done." Kyla then moves to a counter, opens a cabinet, and retrieves a small item before turning to face the camera. A female voiceover narrates Kyla's struggle, stating she gave notice and, despite having five months, has unsuccessfully applied for dozens of properties due to skyrocketing rental prices across Australia. Kyla then speaks directly to the camera, describing her experience as "feeling like you're drowning," where you're "just about to break the surface" but "something grabs your ankle and rips you back down." She concludes by stating, "my kids are definitely worth it," just as the baby, who has been calm, lets out a cry. Kyla then responds, "Aren't they?" capturing a moment of multitasking and domestic routine in her lived-in, slightly chaotic home environment.

The minute opens with a blonde-haired woman, later identified by a narrator as Kyla Jobson, in a black hoodie and jeans, holding her baby, dressed in a pink knitted sweater, who is heard crying softly. Kyla playfully lifts, rocks, and cuddles the infant close to her chest, while she is heard saying, "It's just exhausting. Yes." The setting is a modest, cluttered room with scattered bags, a stroller, and household items, suggesting a lived-in, temporary space. As somber background music begins, a female narrator explains that having a job in Australia is no longer a guarantee of having a house, and despite working six days a week, Kyla Jobson no longer has a place to call home. Kyla then expresses, "And it just feels like you're going in a circle. All I want is for you guys to have somewhere nice and safe." She moves through the room, carrying the baby toward a doorway, then carefully places the infant into a gray stroller, adjusting the cover and ensuring the baby is secure. The narrator continues, stating that when Kyla leaves this house tonight, she and her kids are moving into a motel room. The scene shifts as Kyla, now wearing a sleeveless black top, is seen bending down to interact with a young child in a red shirt at a doorway with a mesh screen, whom she addresses as "Kain," asking, "Kain, are you ready to quickly, quickly run?" Kain responds, "I'm ready," as he reaches out toward her. Kyla warns, "Your feet will get wet. Okay, well mommy will come back for this stuff then, hey?" Kain then says, "I had a wet too," before Kyla concludes, "I'm gonna hold that," indicating a nurturing interaction amidst their challenging circumstances.

event_blocks.summary: Amidst heavy rain, a Woman with baby, dressed in a sleeveless black top and jeans, leads the Young child in red shirt under a red umbrella, reassuring them with phrases like "Now, mum's gonna walk you out" and "Hold it over our heads" as they exit a doorway with a mesh screen, navigating a white picket fence. The Woman with baby continues to comfort the Young child with "It's okay" and "We hop into your seat" as the rain persists. The scene shifts to a tranquil coastal town bathed in morning light, accompanied by somber instrumental music, before Louise Milligan appears, wearing a black coat and red scarf, to address the camera. Louise Milligan declares, "This is the new face of homelessness in Australia," citing the critical lack of affordable housing and introducing the Four Corners episode 'Decay of the Australian Housing Dream,' explaining that towns like this, once accessible to low to middle-income earners, are no longer affordable. The segment concludes with a title card, 'NO PLACE TO CALL HOME,' featuring the Woman with baby in a dimly lit room, holding the Baby in a pink knitted sweater, underscoring the theme of displacement as the somber music swells.

raw_segments: The segment opens with the distinct sound of heavy rain as a Woman with baby, wearing a sleeveless black top and jeans, carefully guides the Young child in red shirt under a red umbrella. She speaks gently to the child, saying, "Now, mum's gonna walk you out," and "Hold it over our heads," as they step out of a doorway with a mesh screen and navigate through a white picket fence into the rainy yard. The Young child makes soft vocalizations, and the Woman reassures them with "It's okay" and "We hop into your seat" amidst the downpour. The scene then transitions, the rain sounds fading, to a serene morning view of a coastal town, with mist rolling over hills and houses bathed in soft sunlight, accompanied by a somber, reflective instrumental music track. The camera pans across the residential area, showcasing rooftops and greenery. Louise Milligan then appears in a medium shot, standing outdoors with the town in the background, wearing a black coat and a red scarf. She speaks directly to the camera in a clear, grave tone, stating, "This is the new face of homelessness in Australia. The critical lack of affordable and public housing means in towns like this, even if you have a job, there is often simply nowhere to live." She introduces the episode of Four Corners as the 'Decay of the Australian Housing Dream,' explaining that such places "used to be the sort of place that people on low to middle incomes could reasonably expect to find a home. It's not anymore. We investigate what went wrong." The somber instrumental music continues throughout her address and swells as the segment concludes with a title card: 'NO PLACE TO CALL HOME,' featuring the Woman with baby in a dimly lit, stark room, holding the Baby in a pink knitted sweater, emphasizing a sense of displacement and hardship.

event_blocks.summary: A somber title card, 'NO PLACE TO CALL HOME,' opens on a Woman with baby holding a Baby in a pink knitted sweater, accompanied by melancholic acoustic guitar music, crashing waves, and seagulls. The scene shifts to a coastal town and then a modest white house identified as 'Pete’s Place, a drop-in center in Coffs Harbour.' On the porch, a Man in white t-shirt sings folk lyrics like 'Mama's be strong,' while others, including a Man in black hoodie and a Man in a beige shirt and cap, gather. Inside, a Woman in patterned top opens a large refrigerator filled with food containers and speaks with a Woman in dark jacket. A staff member explains the center offers food, showers, laundry, and assistance with rental applications and housing, noting weekly donated meals and support for families experiencing 'rental stress.' Later, Anna Scott, Team Leader at Pete’s Place, explains the center is 'ground zero' for regional homelessness, seeing around 80 people daily, including more families, children, and older Australians, with increasing difficulty in rehousing them. She expresses heartbreak when people in crisis accommodation may not find outcomes. In a communal area with colorful artwork, a fire extinguisher, and an evacuation plan, a woman with dark hair tied back, wearing a black patterned top, assists Jessica, who wears a white v-neck t-shirt, in searching for rentals on a laptop. Jessica, who lost her bakery job due to property renovations, has applied for 50-60 properties over five to six weeks with no success, despite her 28 days of crisis accommodation having expired. The laptop displays listings, one for $260 per week, as Jessica calculates on her notepad. The narrator mentions a lack of vacancies and motel availability, underscoring Jessica's ongoing housing struggle.

A distant coastal town with a prominent white building on a hillside is seen before the camera focuses on a modest white house with a porch, which a female narrator identifies as 'Pete's Place, a drop-in center in Coffs Harbour'. On the porch, a group of people gathers, including a Man in white t-shirt playing an acoustic guitar and singing a folk-like melody with lyrics such as 'Mama's be strong' and 'just can't stay here in heaven'. Anna Scott, identified by on-screen text as Team Leader at Pete’s Place drop-in centre, appears, her dark hair tied back and wearing a black patterned top with a lace neckline. Speaking directly to the camera, she explains that 'Pete's Place is ground zero when it comes to homelessness in this region.' She details the centre's growth, noting that from 20 people a day in 2018, they now see around 80, including 'a lot more families, people with children, people with disabilities,' and 'older Australians.' Anna highlights the increasing difficulty in moving people out of homelessness. The bright, functional setting, with windows, a fire extinguisher, and an evacuation plan, underscores the organized environment. An unseen interviewer asks Anna what it's like to see this 'tide of people coming in who have nowhere to live.' And that's terrifying for them, and I think it's really, really sad.' During her speech, another woman in a dark jacket is seen engaging in conversation with Anna, and a brief cut shows hands typing on a laptop, indicating administrative work, all contributing to an atmosphere of community support and organized activity.

The video opens with a close-up of a silver HP laptop on a wooden table, displaying a property listing. A woman with dark hair tied back, wearing a black patterned top, actively uses the laptop’s trackpad, guiding the interaction. She asks, "Let's have a look. Go price. What are we saying your maximum?" Seated next to her, a woman in a white v-neck t-shirt, later identified by a narrator as Jessica, writes notes with a red pencil, responding, "400 they say is my maximum." The camera pulls back to reveal a bright, communal indoor space, likely a drop-in center, filled with background chatter and other people, including a man in a dark shirt eating and another in a yellow jacket moving around. Colorful artwork adorns the walls, and a refrigerator and multiple computer stations suggest a supportive atmosphere. A narrator explains that "Jessica was working in a bakery when the owner of her rental property decided to renovate and gave her notice." The woman in the patterned top points to the screen, saying, "This one's in town, two bedroom," to which Jessica confirms, "Yeah. 320, 320," and the woman in the patterned top replies, "That's good." The scene transitions to an exterior shot of a modest white house with a blue sign reading 'Pete’s Place Engagement Hub,' surrounded by trees and featuring a ramp for accessibility. Back inside, the camera pans across the room, showing the two women still engaged at the laptop, while a man in a white t-shirt with a black necklace sits at a table eating, with a plate of food and a Coca-Cola bottle in front of him. The narrator then asks Jessica, "how many of these properties have you actually applied for so far?" Jessica replies, "I probably do about 10 a week... About 10. Yeah." The narrator follows up, "So how many in total?" Jessica explains, "Oh, I've been so probably in between 50 and 60, because it's been about five or six weeks now that I've applied for every week." The narrator then asks, "And no luck whatsoever?" Jessica confirms, "No." The focus returns to Jessica, who is now writing calculations on her notepad, with the laptop screen showing a rental listing priced at $260.00 per week. The narrator concludes, "Jessica was entitled to 28 days crisis accommodation. But that's now expired. There was no vacancies in town. There and they have two motels they said that they..." as the audio fades, highlighting Jessica's ongoing struggle to find housing within this community support setting.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins in a communal drop-in center, indicated by the Aboriginal flag and colorful artwork, where the Woman in white v-neck t-shirt is initially seen viewing a laptop displaying a property listing for $260 per week. She then recounts her housing struggles to the camera, explaining she had to call her sister due to lack of accommodation and faced the difficult choice of sleeping in her car while her son slept upstairs. The Woman in blue long-sleeve top inquires about her son's age, learning he is nine, and asks about her feelings as a mother, to which the Woman in white expresses fear of the unknown. She reveals she's paying $110 a night for a small cabin at a caravan park, the cheapest option available. Later, the Woman in white, with expressive hand gestures, explains that if she doesn't secure housing, her son will stay with her sister while she might have to sleep in a tent or swag provided by Pete's Place, stating, "Which that's scary." The Woman in blue listens intently, asking if she ever imagined living in a tent. The Woman in white responds emotionally, stating, "No," and expressing her fear of losing her son, emphasizing, "I don't want to lose my boy" and "that's not fair when I've raised him solely," lamenting working "so hard and to get so far and to have everything gone from." The background shows the Man in a dark shirt eating and the Man in beige shirt near the refrigerator, with the fire extinguisher and evacuation plan visible, while the Narrator identifies the Woman in white as Jessica, who "can't afford to keep paying close to $800..."

She describes the difficult choice of sleeping in the back of her car that night while her son slept upstairs. The woman in blue then asks how that feels as a mother, and the woman in white expresses that it's 'scary to not know what's going to happen the next day.' When asked where her son is sleeping now, she explains she's paid for her own accommodation at a caravan park, emphasizing it's 'the cheapest place I could get.' The woman in blue clarifies if it's 'A cabin?', and the woman in white confirms it's a 'small cabin' costing '$110 a night,' which is 'the cheapest in town.' The background remains active with people moving around, and safety features like a fire extinguisher and evacuation plan are visible. The segment concludes with a male narrator's voice identifying the woman as Jessica, stating she 'can't afford to keep paying close to $800...'

The video captures a deeply emotional and serious conversation between two women in what appears to be a community support center, identifiable by an Aboriginal flag hanging on the wall. The Woman in white v-neck t-shirt, with her hair pulled back, speaks with urgent, expressive hand gestures, her voice conveying significant distress and fear. She explains her precarious housing situation, stating that if she doesn't secure a place, her son will stay with her sister while she might have to sleep in a tent or swag provided by Pete's Place, adding, "Which that's scary." The Woman in blue long-sleeve top listens intently with a serious, empathetic expression, occasionally responding with calm, probing questions, such as asking if the speaker ever imagined living in a tent. The Woman in white responds with a firm, emotional "No," her voice breaking as she reveals her deepest fear: "I don't want to lose my boy." She continues, her voice increasingly distressed, about the injustice of potentially losing her son due to housing instability, emphasizing, "that's not fair when I've raised him solely." Her voice trails off in despair as she laments working "so hard and to get so far and to have everything gone from," highlighting the emotional weight of her struggle, which is further emphasized by alternating over-the-shoulder and close-up camera shots.

event_blocks.summary: A woman in a white v-neck t-shirt, her voice emotional, pleads for help for low-income earners and the unemployed, expressing frustration over the lack of housing and inability to find assistance, while an unseen second woman's voice offers clarifying questions and prompts her to address a new Prime Minister. The communal indoor space, possibly a drop-in center, is indicated by an Aboriginal flag, colorful artwork, a notepad with a red pencil, a refrigerator, and a window with a sun sticker, with a man in a beige shirt and cap and a woman in a dark jacket visible in the background. The scene abruptly shifts to a stormy ocean at dusk with crashing waves and strong wind, followed by a silhouette of a power pole with birds against a twilight sky. Dimly lit, handheld shots then reveal a makeshift encampment in a wooded area with tents, a shopping cart filled with belongings, and debris, where a female narrator explains people are forced to live due to lack of housing. The lighting brightens as a woman with long reddish-brown hair, wearing a blue dress and sunglasses, gets into a white car and drives along a narrow, tree-lined dirt road, described by another speaker as a secluded 'hotspot' where people camp at night. The woman, identified as Sarah Borret from New Horizons, drives a white car along a tree-lined dirt road, discussing 'beach walkers' and 'people camping in the bush,' explaining that people sleep in cars due to unaffordable rent, facing risks of violence and theft. The environment transitions from the dirt road to a suburban street, passing a building with a sea turtle mural, a 'Chelsea Motor Inn' sign, and a building with a 'Welcome' sign, as Sarah heads to a camping store to pick up solar equipment for the homeless community, with the sound of the car driving consistently underscoring her journey.

event_blocks.summary: Inside a moving vehicle, a woman with long reddish-brown hair, wearing a blue long-sleeve top and sunglasses, holds a smartphone displaying a map, with the sounds of a car engine and mentions of "battery packs." Exiting a white car with a 'HYBRID' badge parked outside a 'Fishing Tackle' store, she enters the brightly lit establishment, passing racks of camouflage clothing and a 'Clearance Rack' sign, and greets a male cashier with "Hi. Good, how are you?" Identified as Sarah Borrett, her role has shifted from housing assistance to providing tents for those experiencing homelessness, as she inquires about available tents for people sleeping rough due to a lack of accommodation. The cashier directs her, stating, "Yeah, I can show you down here," and points out a suitable item, which Sarah deems "Perfect." Later, in a sunlit park-like setting, Sara Borrett, identified as 'SARA BORRETT, Team leader, New Horizons,' explains that the housing market in 2011 was vastly different, with an "off-peak season" allowing for "cheaper accommodation" and "reasonable rents" due to landlords seeking tenants. Panoramic views of a coastal suburban area reveal houses, trees, misty hills, and the ocean under an overcast sky, while a voice explains that they previously housed 30 people monthly but now struggle to house 10. The narrative highlights that affordable rentals are a "pipe dream" in Coffs Harbour, with winter vacancy rates at a low 1.5%, compared to 5% a decade ago, and nationwide vacancy rates are at historic lows. The scene shifts to a grassy area with a partially visible green tent among trees, a silver SUV ('DDS 66W') parked near a fence, and a white and orange tent in the background, suggesting temporary encampments. Sara Borrett, standing outdoors in a sunlit park, elaborates that the process for these individuals is lengthy, leading to the provision of tents and many sleeping in their cars. Aerial views of the coastal town return, showing mist over the hills and ocean, and a modern building with a distinctive design.

event_blocks.summary: The video opens with a static shot of a modest white house, transitioning to a close-up of Sara Borrett, identified as 'Team leader, New Horizons,' speaking from a park about the issue of overcrowding and extended families living together. As melancholic instrumental music begins, a sweeping aerial view of a coastal suburban area at sunset is shown, with the sun casting a golden glow. Another woman, wearing a dark blue V-neck top, speaks in a grassy area, explaining that the housing crisis in Coffs Harbour affects towns nationwide due to pressures like lack of affordable housing, COVID migration, and the Airbnb market, leading to a dire lack of rental homes and rising prices. The scene then shifts to the exterior of a 'PRD. REAL ESTATE' office with a red awning, displaying property listings marked 'SOLD,' and a 'Rent Smarter' sign. A male narrator states that weekly rents in Coffs Harbour have risen significantly, almost double the rate of the rest of New South Wales, while aerial shots showcase dense residential neighborhoods with solar panels. A female speaker explains that people are choosing Coffs Harbour for its affordability and remote work opportunities. Inside a modern real estate office with a 'national REAL ESTATE Coffs Coast' banner, Lisa Hanlan, in a blue sleeveless top, guides a younger woman in a black blazer as they process green forms, with the narrator explaining that demand for rentals took off during the COVID pandemic, leading to numerous applications per listing. Lisa Hanlan further elaborates on the difficulty genuine people with good references and income face in securing rentals due to high competition, impacting professionals like doctors and nurses. The video concludes with aerial shots of the coastal town at sunset and a panoramic view of the coastline with crashing waves, as the narrator begins to cite data on housing stress in Coffs Harbour.

event_blocks.summary: Sweeping aerial drone shots of a coastal suburban area during golden hour reveal dense residential housing, modern apartment buildings, and distant ocean views, as a narrator discusses the affordable housing crisis for low-income individuals in Coffs Harbour, noting a decade-long waitlist for government homes and the struggle to find private rentals. The scene shifts to dynamic ocean waves crashing on a rocky shore, followed by a wide view of a sandy beach, underscoring the narrator's point that many homes remain empty despite desperate need. The narrative then focuses on Stacy Warn, a local disability worker, pushing a stroller with two young children, one in a red shirt and another in a purple outfit, accompanied by a golden retriever on a quiet suburban street. As they walk past green lawns, trees, and trash bins under bright sunlight, the dog pants and barks, and a child's voice is heard saying "Okay, let's go" and "Bye bye." The tour continues past a modest brick house with a green roof, identified as social housing, and then highlights several houses in disrepair: one empty for at least eight months, another with a blue tarp roof, and a third with a damaged roof covered by a white tarp due to persistent leaks. Further down the street, a house with broken furniture and debris on its lawn, and another with a boarded window, are also noted as long-term empty properties. The narrator explains that many occupied homes are run-down and overcrowded, citing an example of a family of six in a three-bedroom house, a stark contrast to the calm demeanor of Stacy Warn amidst the visible signs of community struggle and makeshift repairs.

raw_segments: The minute opens with sweeping aerial drone shots of a coastal suburban area during golden hour, showcasing dense residential housing nestled among lush green trees, with distant hills and the ocean visible under a hazy sky. The camera glides over rooftops, capturing solar panels and varied architectural styles, before panning to reveal modern apartment buildings. Over melancholic background music, a female narrator begins to speak about affordable one-bedroom homes for people on a low income. The scene transitions to a close-up of a woman with dark hair, wearing a dark green V-neck top, speaking directly to the camera in a grassy outdoor setting. She continues the narration, detailing the housing crisis in Coffs Harbour, explaining that low-income individuals struggle to find affordable private rentals, public housing isn't an option, and the waitlist for a government home exceeds 10 years. The footage then cuts to dynamic shots of the ocean, with waves crashing onto a rocky shore under a partly cloudy sky, followed by a wide view of a sandy beach with a high-rise building in the distance, as the narrator concludes that despite desperate need, many homes sit empty. The final sequence shows a woman with blonde hair tied in a bun, wearing a black lace top, pushing a stroller with two young children—one in a red shirt and one in a purple outfit—along a quiet suburban street. A golden retriever walks beside them, with sounds of the dog panting and a child's voice saying "Okay, let's go" and "Bye bye." The camera follows them from behind as they move down the road, passing green lawns, trees, and trash bins, under bright sunlight with long shadows. The narrator identifies this woman as local disability worker Stacy Warn, who is giving a tour of the estate where she lives with her four children and her dad, as the dog barks.

The video opens with a wide shot of a modest brick house with a green roof, which a female narrator identifies as part of the social housing in the area. The camera then follows a woman with blonde hair in a bun, wearing a black lace top and dark shorts, as she pushes a stroller with two young children along a sunlit suburban street, accompanied by a golden retriever. The distinct sounds of the stroller and footsteps are heard, and one of the children briefly calls out "Didi." As they move, the camera pans to reveal various houses in varying states of disrepair, highlighting a stark contrast. The narrator points out a house that has been empty for at least eight months, noting it's a three-bedroom house with a good backyard, but nothing has been done to it, which is "really sad." Other houses show visible signs of hardship: one with a blue tarp covering its roof and another with a damaged roof and white tarp, which the narrator explains is due to residents tying tarpaulins over them to keep out rain from bad leaks, as damage still comes through every time it rains. Further down, a house with broken furniture and debris scattered on the lawn, and another with a boarded window, are described by the narrator as empty homes that have been left for a long time, not even getting cleaned. The narrator adds that many of the lived-in homes are run-down and overcrowded, citing an example of a family of six living in a three-bedroom place. The woman’s calm demeanor contrasts with the visible signs of community struggle and adaptation, emphasized by the makeshift coverings and boarded windows, all set against a backdrop of bright sunlight casting long shadows.

event_blocks.summary: In a sunlit suburban backyard, a woman with dark hair in a bun described her family's cramped living situation in a "small three bedroom" house where "all three kids, two boys and one girl are crammed in one tiny room," with "the two of them are additional needs." She detailed numerous house problems, including only "get one hot shower a day" for "six of us," a "roof's falling in at the back," a backyard that "floods, it doesn't drain," leading to "maggots 'cause the sewage comes up all through the backyard," and "holes in the floor" that took "six, eight months before it got fixed," concluding with resignation, "nothing we can do about it. It's a roof over our head." A blonde woman, wearing a black lace top, listened and agreed, "Yeah. And that's how we all have to, we all have to look at it," explaining that "we ring maintenance, nothing happens, then if we get, if we look into getting things done about it, we get in trouble for for fixing it. So we just have to deal with it." Amidst sounds of children playing and birds chirping, the blonde woman, identified as STACEY WARN, pushed a black stroller with two young children—one in a red shirt and one in a purple outfit—down a sunny suburban street, accompanied by a golden retriever. The street featured green lawns, trees, houses with damaged roofs (one with a blue tarp), and trash bins, with long shadows cast on the pavement. A narrator introduced Stacey as having "a lot of pluck," a "stable job," and being "desperately trying to get out," having "applied unsuccessfully for dozens of private rental properties." Stacey, a "Disability support worker," spoke directly to the camera, expressing her desire to "hop out of social housing" and stating, "I've gone and I've gotten a job, I work three days a week now, and the other three days I do community work." She felt she was "looked at and characterized" as a "single mum of four kids," finding it "really unfair" despite her hard work as a disability support worker.

event_blocks.summary: STACEY WARN, a woman with blonde hair in a bun, wearing a black lace top and sunglasses, speaks directly to the camera in a sunlit suburban setting, explaining that homeless individuals seek her help, but she believes that without hope, there is no solution, regardless of support letters or personal savings, stating that being in social housing feels like being stuck. Somber background music begins as the scene transitions to aerial drone footage of a coastal suburban neighborhood in the center of Coffs Harbour, specifically the Argyle Estate. The drone glides over single-family homes with tree-lined streets, revealing a mix of roof conditions, including solar panels, damaged roofs covered with blue and white tarps, houses with debris on lawns, and boarded windows. A female narrator identifies the Argyle Estate as having 129 social housing homes and 68 privately owned properties, and introduces Local Aboriginal elder Evette Pacy as an advocate for some residents.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of STACEY WARN, a woman with blonde hair in a bun, wearing a black lace top and sunglasses on her head, speaking directly to the camera in a sunlit suburban setting with green foliage in the background. With a serious and engaged tone, STACEY shares her experience, stating that people who become homeless come to her, and while she sends them on "all the avenues," she knows deep down that if she has no hope, neither do they. She describes it as "really sad" and a journey where "there's no solution," emphasizing that "it doesn't matter how many support letters you get, what you have behind your back." STACEY reveals, "I've even got savings. I've tried my hardest to save as much as I can, and it doesn't make any difference. It's just being in social housing, it's like you're stuck here." As somber background music begins, the scene transitions to sweeping aerial drone footage of a coastal suburban neighborhood. The camera glides smoothly over a dense residential area with single-family homes, tree-lined streets, and a mix of roof types, including solar panels and damaged roofs covered with blue and white tarps, some houses showing debris on lawns or boarded windows. A female narrator's voice overlays these visuals, identifying the location as "the Argyle Estate" in the center of Coffs Harbour. She details that the estate currently has "129 social housing homes and 68 privately owned properties," and introduces "Local Aboriginal elder Evette Pacy" as "an advocate for some of the residents in the estate," as the aerial view continues to showcase the varied conditions of the homes.

event_blocks.summary: In a quiet suburban neighborhood on ARGYLL ST, bathed in golden late-afternoon light, children play basketball barefoot near modest houses, while Yvette Pacey, Chair of the Garlambirra Guuyu-Girwaa Aboriginal Elders group, explains that the area is home to established Indigenous and non-Indigenous families who have lived there for generations. A woman in a wide-brimmed hat walks with a young boy in a navy school uniform, depicting routine community life. However, a narrator reveals these homes are slated for demolition and rebuilding under a NSW housing scheme, 'Communities Plus,' which requires developers to include only 30% social housing, creating significantly more private homes than social housing properties, with residents reportedly not informed. Narelle, an Argyll Estate resident since 1997, expresses deep concern and confusion about the development, stating, 'this is my home, my grandkids' home,' and that she 'just can't leave.' She and Yvette Pacey discuss the lack of clear information regarding relocation during redevelopment, with Narelle questioning where they will go and for how long, describing the situation as 'confusing.'

She stands beside Narelle, listening intently and responding with a clear, inquisitive voice, asking probing questions about the development and the residents' feelings, occasionally nodding or touching her face.

raw_segments: The video opens with an aerial shot of a quiet suburban neighborhood, showcasing a street lined with modest homes, green lawns, and trees under bright daylight. The camera then transitions to a close-up of Yvette Pacey, identified as Chair of the Garlambirra Guuyu-Girwaa Aboriginal Elders group, standing on a grassy verge. Wearing a colorful floral dress and sunglasses, she speaks directly to the camera with a serious, clear tone, explaining that the area is home to well-established Indigenous and non-Indigenous families who have lived there for generations, raised families, and benefited from the community, considering it their home. As she speaks, a subtle, melancholic background music begins to play. The scene shifts to children playing basketball barefoot on the street between green wheeled trash bins, and the camera pans across various single-story houses with different architectural styles, some with palm trees and front porches, all bathed in the warm golden light of late afternoon, reinforcing the sense of a peaceful, lived-in community. A street sign for 'ARGYLL ST' is visible, followed by a woman in a wide-brimmed hat walking hand-in-hand with a young boy in a navy school uniform, depicting routine community life. A narrator's voice then overlays the scene, revealing a critical development: these very homes are slated for demolition and rebuilding under a New South Wales housing scheme called 'Communities Plus.' The narrator highlights that this scheme only requires developers to include 30% social housing, with plans to create 254 extra private homes but only nine additional social housing properties, implying a significant shift away from the existing community's needs. The narrator concludes, stating that 'Residents haven't been told...', leaving the sentence unfinished and underscoring a lack of transparency.

The video opens with a wide shot of a suburban street bathed in golden late-afternoon light, where two young boys play basketball barefoot on a grassy verge near a modest house with a white car. The camera then pans to a basketball hoop, as a female narrator discusses how people will be housed while development takes place. The scene transitions to a close-up of Yvette Pacey, identified as Chair of the Garlambirra Guuyu-Girwaa Aboriginal Elders group, with curly blonde hair and a vibrant floral dress, speaking directly to the camera from a grassy verge. She expresses deep concern about a 10-year waiting list for social housing in Coffs Harbour, questioning where people will be relocated during development, especially with existing housing and accommodation shortages. Yvette highlights the significant impact these proposed changes have on people's mental health, anxiety, and confusion about their future, with distant traffic sounds audible in the background. The camera then cuts to a wide shot of a single-story white house, decorated with flamingo statues and potted plants, where a white car is parked. A woman in a pink long-sleeve top and blue jeans exits the house and tends to plants in the sunlit front garden, as a distinct "whoosh" sound and a soft "uh-huh" are heard. A narrator then introduces Narelle, stating she has raised her children, grandchildren, and great-grandchildren on the Arago.

The video opens with a close-up of a woman, later identified as Narelle, in a pink long-sleeve top and blue jeans, diligently tending to a garden bed. She uses a small rake to clear mulch around a plant, with a solar-powered garden light and a decorative gnome nearby. She then bends to inspect a blue artificial rose and other garden ornaments, including a small windmill. As she stands and walks toward the camera, her voice is heard, stating her long-standing residency in the 'Argyll Estate since 1997.' The scene shifts to a medium shot of Narelle, her long, dark hair blowing in the wind, speaking with a concerned expression. On-screen text identifies her as 'NARELLE, Argyll Estate resident.' She emphasizes, in a somewhat gravelly but clear voice, that this is 'my home, my grandkids' home,' and having been there 'all them years, I just can't leave. I can't, I don't want to leave.' She speaks of knowing 'everyone here' and how 'we all sort of stick together.' She is then joined by Yvette Pacey, a woman with curly blonde hair in a colorful floral dress, who listens intently. Yvette, with a clear, inquisitive voice, asks Narelle, 'What do you know about this development that's happening?' Narelle responds that 'they have sent out letters, but some letters I can't really understand.' Yvette further probes, 'Have they given you any indication when this area's being redeveloped, where you will go in the meantime?' Narelle emphatically replies 'No,' expressing deep concern and confusion: 'Now what if they put us into a motel? And they're doing all this, how do we know how long we're going to stay there for? How do we know where we're going to go next?' Yvette nods and occasionally touches her face as Narelle gestures, her voice conveying the emotional impact. Yvette then asks, 'What's the feeling?' to which Narelle concludes, 'It's um confusing, you know what I mean?' The two women stand side-by-side in a sunlit suburban driveway, with a black SUV and a white car in the background, engaged in this serious conversation in a quiet residential area.

event_blocks.summary: Narelle, an Argyll Estate resident, stood in a sunlit suburban driveway, her pink long-sleeve top contrasting with her dark hair as she spoke with expressive gestures, conveying deep hurt and reluctance to leave due to cherished memories, while a black SUV with a roof rack was parked nearby under a clear blue sky. The scene shifted to a shaded backyard where Tia, another resident, walked with a baby in a light purple outfit, playfully interacting with the child who babbled and put fingers in her mouth. Tia was later seen inside a modest white house, holding the baby with red curtains visible, as a narrator explained her role in raising grandchildren on the estate. In a cluttered bedroom, Tia, wearing a gray knitted cardigan, expressed frustration about the limited number of new houses, calling the situation "ropable" and un-Australian. As she moved through a hallway, pointing out her bathroom, she was joined by Yvette Pacey and another woman in a dark blue top. A narrator revealed Tia had mental health issues and waited 12 years for social housing, obtaining her current home eight years prior. Entering a child’s bedroom with a blue bedspread and a stuffed bear, Tia firmly stated she would not leave her home, emphasizing her long wait and struggles, and her need to be there for her grandchildren and daughter. The camera then focused on a white cockatiel in a cage near an orange tiger tapestry, a dreamcatcher, and framed photos, highlighting the personal and family-oriented nature of the space. Later, Tia spoke with Louise Milligan, who wore a dark blue V-neck top and listened intently, expressing concern about the disproportionate increase in houses versus public housing availability, stating it was "not right" and that public housing should be prioritized.

The scene transitions to a shaded backyard where Tia, another Argyll Estate resident, walks with a baby in a light purple outfit. Tia's playful voice is heard as she interacts with the baby, asking, "Can you say, 'wee'?" and noting, "You got no shoes on," while the baby, with curly hair tied in a small ponytail, looks around curiously, occasionally putting fingers in her mouth, and making distinct babbling sounds. The minute opens with a close-up of TIA, an Argyll Estate resident in a gray knitted cardigan, speaking earnestly in a warmly lit, cluttered bedroom. Her serious expression and gestures convey a personal, emotional narrative. An interviewer's voice asks how she feels about only nine more houses, to which TIA responds with a voice full of frustration, declaring, "Oh, ropable. Ropable," adding, "That's not what we are as Australians." As she moves through a narrow hallway, pointing out "my bathroom," she is joined by YVETTE PACEY, with curly blonde hair, a colorful floral dress, and sunglasses, and another woman in a dark blue top, both listening intently. A narrator then provides crucial context, explaining that TIA has mental health issues and was on the social housing list for 12 years before finally getting this home eight years ago. They enter a child’s bedroom, featuring a blue bedspread, a stuffed bear, and a window overlooking a sunlit backyard with trees and a trampoline. TIA continues speaking, her tone animated and deeply emotional, responding to a question about leaving by firmly stating, "No. They'll have to cut me out... I'm just not going anywhere." She elaborates on her struggles, her voice cracking with emotion, "I waited a long time. I've gone through too much in life," emphasizing her commitment: "If I'm not here to help with my grandchildren and help my daughter through this, things don't always work out the way we dream when we're young." The camera pans to reveal a white cockatiel in a metal cage near a vibrant orange tiger tapestry and a small fan, amidst personal items like a dreamcatcher and framed photos, all within the intimate, family-oriented space bathed in natural, warm lighting. TIA's urgent and concerned voice underscores her deep connection to her home and family support.

event_blocks.summary: TIA, an Argyll Estate resident, speaking from her cluttered home in a gray knitted cardigan, declared her intention to fight for her housing situation, stating, "whatever is like everything. So I'm not going anywhere. Not without a fight. That's fight." The scene shifted to a coastal sunset with powerful waves crashing against rocks, followed by a silhouette of a person walking along a fenced pathway and a wide shot of a coastal suburban town bathed in late afternoon sunlight. A narrator explained that the Coffs Harbour City Council sought answers from the New South Wales Land and Housing Corporation regarding the future of residents like Tia. Tony Judge, a Coffs Harbour City Councillor, speaking outdoors in a garden-like setting, stated that "currently you've got about 60% of that development is social housing. It's not dysfunctional, it's not a problem area," and that Council had inquired about additional social housing in the Argyll Estate redevelopment. He was briefly seen conversing with a woman in a black coat and pink scarf before continuing his discussion about maintaining social housing levels. Judge later expressed shock and concern outdoors, gesturing subtly, over the Argyle estate development, noting there was "no commitment that there'd be more social housing" and "no criteria... to say this has to be affordable housing," asserting the land release had been "missold." The camera alternated between close-ups of Judge and over-the-shoulder shots of the woman in the black coat and pink scarf, who listened intently and asked a question. The narrator revealed the New South Wales Land and Housing Corporation would not confirm the number of social housing homes in the new Argyle development. Sweeping aerial views of a coastal suburban town at sunrise or sunset followed, with golden light illuminating residential housing and the ocean, accompanied by a bird chirping. The segment concluded with a focus on a red-brick apartment building with parked cars, where a yellow 'ALLEGEDLY' sign was partially visible.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins in the grand lobby of Australian Parliament House, where a woman in a dark blue blazer, later identified as Louise Milligan, walks towards a conference room, her movement captured from an overhead perspective highlighting the geometric floor. Inside a well-lit office, Milligan meets with Julie Collins, the Federal Housing Minister, who is wearing a gray plaid blazer over a pink top with a blue flower pin. Collins expresses her lifelong passion for housing, stemming from her childhood in public housing, and discusses the current housing stress. She critiques the previous government for insufficient action on social and affordable housing and inadequate collaboration with state and territory ministers. The conversation shifts to a housing ministers' meeting, the first in nearly five years, highlighting the urgency of the situation. Collins outlines her government's commitment to building 30,000 social and affordable homes through the Housing Australia Future Fund. A panoramic view of a coastal suburban town with dense housing and the ocean is shown, accompanied by narration about the appeal from housing stakeholders to build 25,000 homes annually to address a backlog. The scene briefly shows a modest brick house and a street with a 'KEEP LEFT' sign before returning to the interview. Milligan questions if the 30,000-home target is sufficient, suggesting it should be tripled, to which Collins acknowledges construction sector constraints but reaffirms the commitment to build homes rapidly. Milligan then poignantly describes the plight of homeless working Australians, including nurses and retail workers, who are couch surfing or living in dilapidated housing, questioning what they are supposed to do in the interim. Collins responds with empathy, acknowledging the heartbreaking nature of these stories, which she has heard throughout her 13 years as a Member of Parliament.

Her clear, professional voice narrates the segment, introducing the purpose of the visit and Julie Collins, and also engages in an initial exchange upon entering the office.

Julie Collins then critiques the previous government, asserting they "clearly didn't do enough when it came to getting more stock of social and affordable houses on the ground" and "didn't work with state and territory ministers enough." The video segment features an interview-style conversation in an indoor office setting with soft, even lighting, creating a calm and serious atmosphere. Julie Collins, the Minister for Housing and Homelessness, is seen in a close-up, wearing a gray plaid blazer over a pink top, a silver circular pendant necklace, and a blue flower pin on her lapel, her hair pulled back. She speaks earnestly, using expressive hand gestures, and is first heard discussing the need for collective effort and a national housing and homelessness plan to get "more affordable homes on the ground quickly." I've been hearing those stories myself," and mentions her 13 years as a Member of Parliament hearing similar accounts. The background alternates between wooden shelves behind Julie and blinds behind Louise, maintaining focus on their facial expressions and gestures throughout the grave discussion.

event_blocks.summary: Julie Collins, Minister for Housing and Homelessness, expressed concern over distressing stories heard in her electoral offices, stating efforts to move quickly. Uplifting instrumental music accompanied aerial views of a rural landscape before transitioning to a small town, as a narrator explained that Kaila Jobson is forced to live in motels. Kaila, a woman with blonde hair, carried a baby in a floral headband down a hospital hallway, followed by a young child in a blue and black 'NSW' jacket. The child said 'Morning, TV,' and Kaila responded, 'Yeah, well you can run if you want to. But be careful.' Exiting through an 'ENTRANCE' door and descending a metal walkway with blue railings, Kaila asked, 'You gonna hold my hand?' to which the child replied 'Morning!' before Kaila said 'Okay.' Kaila later stated she had applied unsuccessfully for 100 properties, with the child counting 'One, two, three' while descending stairs past a 'DIGGERS TAVERN' and 'MOTEL ACCOMMODATION COFFEE BAR' sign. In a softly lit room, Kaila expressed frustration over rising rental prices, noting two-bedroom houses increased from "350 a week" to "650, 700 a week," while her income was "about 750 after tax." Descending a dimly lit staircase, the child exclaimed, "It's a bad doctor there!" and "No, to be scared," but Kaila reassured, "It's okay," and "No, we're here now." They entered a laundry room where Kaila loaded a Fisher & Paykel QuickSmart washing machine, placing a laundry basket on a red table as the child, now calmer, watched. The narrator explained Kaila was living in a room behind the tavern where she works as a cleaner and at the bar, with her boss helping with a cheaper room. Kaila reflected on her past homelessness with her siblings and mother for eight months, vowing never to be in that position with her own children, and now understanding her mother's efforts. She spoke of feeling "really sick" and "really crap" about her struggles, having to "constantly reassure" her son that their situation was "just a new adventure." In a cluttered kitchen, Kaila washed a garment at the sink while a child cried, asking, "Mummy, you done the TV?" Kaila gently reassured, "Won't be for long. It's only super quick... I'll be right there, it's okay." The child, wearing a blue and black 'NSW' jacket, stood near a doorway in a hallway, looking down pensively. The child cried and whined, and Kaila attempted to soothe them, asking, "Do you want to help me?" and reassuring, "It's okay," and "Hey, it's all right." In the kitchen, the child refused to sit in the spare room, saying "No," and later "No, I will" when asked to sit on the floor to help with laundry. Kaila explained, "There's no lunch at the moment. You can sit on the lounge when Mommy goes behind the bar, yeah?" before instructing the child to "play with Alex for one minute, while Mommy runs up the hallway and helps Peta." They headed toward an illuminated green 'EXIT' sign.

She shares a personal story about her past, recalling a time when her family lived "kind of rougher" after her parents split, leading to her and her siblings being homeless with her mom for about eight months, living in the same motel for a few months while trying to find a place. She remembers swearing she'd never be in that position if she had kids, and now realizes her mom was "trying so hard to just have somewhere safe for us and get us a house," expressing regret for not understanding her mother's efforts at the time.

The minute opens in a softly lit bedroom where Kaila Jobson, a woman with blonde hair in a white top and dark cardigan, sits on a bed, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. Her voice is thoughtful and somber as she reflects on feeling "really sick" and "really crap" about personal struggles, specifically how things "crumbled" despite her efforts to provide a better life for her children. Behind her, a stuffed bear sits on a patterned pillow, adding to the intimate atmosphere. She explains having to "constantly reassure" her son that their new situation is "just a new adventure," adopting a forced cheerful tone to make it seem like she was "really excited" about living at "Mummy's work" so he could be with her. The scene briefly cuts to a close-up of a cardboard box filled with children’s toys, including a green caterpillar and a red Spider-Man figure, before returning to Kaila. The setting then shifts to a cluttered kitchen where Kaila, now in a black t-shirt with striped sleeves, is seen washing a dark garment at the sink under a bright window with a sun sticker. Simultaneously, the distressed cries and whines of a young child are heard, asking, "Mummy, you done the TV?" Kaila's voice immediately becomes gentle and reassuring, promising, "Won't be for long. It's only super quick... I'll be right there, it's okay." The final visual shows the young child, wearing a blue and black 'NSW' jacket, standing near a doorway in a hallway, looking down pensively before turning slightly, their quiet emotional presence underscored by their continued soft crying as Kaila comforts them.

The minute opens with a close-up of a young child in a blue and black 'NSW' jacket, looking down pensive near a doorway, accompanied by the distinct sound of their crying and whining. A woman with long blonde hair, who identifies herself as "Mommy," attempts to soothe the child, asking "Do you want to help me?" and later reassuring, "It's okay," and "Hey, it's all right," amidst the child's continued, more intense crying, punctuated by a shushing sound. The scene shifts to a modestly furnished kitchen where Mommy bends to assist the child, who is now holding a small object. The child's young voice is heard saying "Me too," followed by a defiant "No" when Mommy instructs them to sit in the spare room. A clattering sound is heard, prompting Mommy to exclaim "Jesus" quietly, before she explains, "That way I can hear." The child again defiantly says "No." Mommy then pushes a gray stroller containing a baby with a floral headband, while the child walks beside her, holding a colorful toy. As they move through the kitchen into a hallway with red carpeting and white walls, passing a 'KEY RETURN' slot and a notice board, Mommy warns, "Watch out, watch your toes." Mommy asks the child, "Are you going to sit on the floor for me, so Mommy can help get all the laundry with Peta?" The child responds with a whining "No, I will." Mommy further explains, "There's no lunch at the moment. You can sit on the lounge when Mommy goes behind the bar, yeah?" before concluding, "All right, you just play with Alex for one minute, while Mommy runs up the hallway and helps Peta." They head toward an illuminated green 'EXIT' sign, with the bright, natural lighting and institutional feel of the hallway suggesting a temporary or shared living space, capturing a calm, routine moment of care despite the child's distress.

event_blocks.summary: In a long, narrow hallway with red carpeting and white walls, a woman with blonde hair, wearing a dark blue cardigan, carried two large white laundry bags, assisted by another woman in a dark top and jeans. Amidst the sound of a baby crying loudly, the blonde woman exclaimed, "Oh god." They deposited the bags near a 'PRIVATE' door and entered a room where a gray stroller held a baby, Alex, dressed in a light gray outfit with a floral headband, smiling and playing with toys. The scene shifted to a softly lit bedroom where the blonde woman sat on a bed, speaking to the camera. Responding to a male voice asking about the practical impact of not having a home, she stated, "Everything," detailing her limited cooking facilities—a microwave, fridge, toaster, and kettle—which she must use for the children's meals or supplement with purchased food. The baby's crying resumed, and she addressed "Alex," expressing her constant anxiety, particularly at night, due to the baby's young age and her fear of frustrating hotel customers, which could jeopardize her accommodation and job. She voiced her desire to avoid appearing annoying or inconvenient, emphasizing her heightened state of unease. The baby's prolonged crying intensified her distress as she concluded, "I already don't have a house. If I then lost my job, there would be no..." The video returned to the hallway, showing the two women moving the laundry bags towards a communal area with a notice board and 'KEY RETURN' slot, leading to an exterior metal walkway with blue railings, illustrating the routine and shared responsibilities within their transitional living situation.

event_blocks.summary: A street-level view of a bustling, gentrified Bellingen, alive with the sounds of moving vehicles and pedestrians, showcases vibrant storefronts including an outdoor café with dining patrons and a clothing store with stylish mannequins, reflecting a new demographic that sips lattes and browses artisan homewares. Amidst this activity, the narrator notes the growing housing crisis, stating that "more and more of the people on lower wages who keep this town going have nowhere to live." The scene broadens to reveal historic architecture like 'The Commercial Emporium' and 'Hammond Wheatley Drapery,' before aerial shots display the town nestled in a lush valley with a winding river, green fields, and distant mountains, accompanied by melancholic background music. The narrator elaborates on the housing crisis, reporting that "Rents went up in the Bellingen area by 48% in the year to December 2021, the highest increase in New South Wales," which is "three times the average rent rise in regional Australia... and 16 times higher than the increase in capital cities." The narrative concludes indoors in a softly lit bedroom, where a woman with long blonde hair, wearing a dark cardigan and with a stuffed bear and patterned bedding visible behind her, speaks directly to the camera with an earnest expression, her voice, initially a brief "nothing," returning to convey the emotional strain: "It's starting to get a bit overwhelming, but it's just you just gotta push through it. It's just there's no other..."

event_blocks.summary: In a softly lit bedroom, a woman with long blonde hair, dressed in a dark blue cardigan over a white top, articulates her coping mechanisms, emphasizing the need to "keep putting one foot in front of the other" despite exhaustion and the mental effort of planning for the next day. She reveals a strategy of emotional suppression to maintain functionality, stating, "I'm just not letting myself feel anything too much because then I'll just break down, I won't be able to handle it and the kids need me to be able to be on top of my game right now," humorously scheduling a "mental breakdown for later in the year." The scene shifts to a dimly lit bar environment where a bartender with a tattooed arm pours white wine amidst sounds of liquid pouring and background chatter. Later, the same woman, now in a black shirt and striped apron, efficiently serves a beer to a customer, processing a card payment and confirming, "There we go. Just on card? 17 altogether. Perfect. Thank you." Concurrently, a baby in a gray stroller, wearing a floral headband, is seen smiling and playing with toys in a communal area. A deeper female voice narrates, "This little family is not asking for much," and poses a question about experience. The bartender continues her work, wiping the counter and organizing glasses in the lively bar, with persistent sounds of pouring liquid, clinking glasses, and chatter.

event_blocks.summary: A young boy in a green hoodie is shown in a dimly lit room, looking at a smartphone and a table laden with food and drinks, as a female voiceover muses on the "raw truth" and its implications for the country. The scene shifts to a vibrant bar environment where a blonde woman, dressed in a black shirt and striped apron, works behind the bar. She operates a card reader, engages with patrons, and moves through the well-lit space, which includes wooden floors, tables, chairs, a pool table, various beer taps labeled 'BALTER' and 'GREAT NORTHERN', a Coca-Cola tap, and shelves stocked with bottles. She sets a glass on a table before proceeding towards the exit, passing a hand sanitizing station marked with a red 'STOP!' sign, and exits through glass doors. Subsequently, the setting changes to a softly lit bedroom where the same woman, now in a white top and dark cardigan, sits on a bed next to a stuffed bear. Addressing the camera with a serious, emotional demeanor, she expresses her distress, stating, "It just sucks. I don't want a handout or anything. It feels crap. I feel like I'm a good person, I don't break the law. I work all the time, I'll help anyone anytime, anywhere. All I just want is somewhere for me and the kids to be able to call our home. I want people to understand that it's not sustainable to be able to live like this." She elaborates on the impossibility of her situation, emphasizing, "And it's just impossible, like, it's really impossible. Something needs to happen where there's not so many people in this position, because it's, yeah, too much." The video concludes with end credits rolling over a stylized orange '4' logo, detailing production credits for Louise Milligan, Ali Russell, and Simon Brynjolffsen, all set against an upbeat, dramatic orchestral score.
